segments.title: Doors Opening: England, France, and the River
supplementalSources: British Library — Henry VIII and the Reformation
Canadian Encyclopedia — Jacques Cartier, Stadacona
Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy — Martin Luther

blocks.text: The story does not begin with our people. It begins with the doors they would later walk through — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river in a country no Englishman yet called home.

In 1485, on a Leicestershire field at Bosworth, the last Plantagenet king fell and a Welsh-born Tudor lifted a crown from the mud. Henry VII meant to end the Wars of the Roses; what he really did was hand his teenage son a restless island that would spend the next century tearing at its own faith.

That son became Henry VIII in 1509 — eighteen years old, gifted, vain, and hungry for a male heir. England was still Catholic then, still Latin in its liturgy, still paying its conscience to Rome. Within a generation all of that would crack.

Across the Channel in 1517, a German monk named Martin Luther hammered his objections to indulgences onto a church door in Wittenberg. He had read the Latin Bible until the words burned in his hands. Rome ordered his books burned in 1521; by then the argument could not be unlit. The Protestant Reformation was not a single explosion — it was a slow fire moving through northern Europe, reaching into English parishes and French towns alike.

While England wrestled with conscience, France looked west. In 1534, navigator Jacques Cartier sailed the Gulf of St. Lawrence and climbed the great river toward a fortified Iroquois town the locals called Stadacona. He heard another word — kanata, village — and claimed the valley for Francis I. France had a doorway of its own now, colder and narrower than Spain's gold coast, but real.

Henry VIII's break with Rome was as political as it was spiritual. Denied an annulment, he made himself head of the English Church in 1534 and, between 1536 and 1541, dissolved the monasteries — brick by brick, chalice by chalice — to fill the crown's empty coffers. Abbeys that had stood for centuries became stone quarries for manor houses. Ordinary people watched saints' days disappear from the calendar and wondered what would replace them.

Death kept reshuffling the deck. James V of Scotland died in 1542, leaving a week-old daughter, Mary, to rule a kingdom. Henry VIII died in 1547, leaving nine-year-old Edward VI to the protectorship of ambitious men. In 1549 the Church of England printed its Book of Common Prayer in English — not Latin — and Parliament demanded uniformity. Faith became something you could be fined for.

Edward's death in 1553 brought nine days of Lady Jane Grey and then the return of Catholic Mary — fires in Smithfield, exiles on the Continent, names scratched from wills. John Calvin's Geneva writings crossed the sea in 1555, sharpening Puritan minds that already thought England's settlement had not gone far enough. When Mary died in 1558, Elizabeth Tudor inherited a country split down the middle.

Elizabeth's answer was compromise worn like armor: the Act of Supremacy again, an Oath that could ruin you, a church that kept some old robes while preaching salvation by faith. By the 1570s she was an aging queen on a contested throne, granting patents to men willing to risk ships on North Atlantic fog. England was learning to look past its own shoreline. France was already building on the St. Lawrence. Two colonial stories had begun — separate, unaware that centuries later they would meet in an Iowa wedding.

segments.title: Two Doorways: Hébert and Alcock
supplementalSources: Dictionary of Canadian Biography — Louis Hébert
Parks Canada — Port-Royal / early Acadian settlement
Cambridgeshire parish history — Protestant ministry in the late 1500s

blocks.text: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight.

Six years later and a hundred leagues north across the sea, another birth quietly mattered as much. On 25 March 1581, George Alcock came into the world at Cambridge in Cambridgeshire, son of Protestant minister John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily Abrich. No crown noted the day. No patent was issued. But the Powers branch of this tree — the English line that would one day meet the Hébert line in Iowa — starts here, in a university town smelling of river mud and ink.

France was filling the St. Lawrence with names our cousins would carry. In Perche, Abraham Côsté was born in 1576; in La Rochelle, Jean Galleran Martin — called the Merchant of Metz — would father children who crossed the Atlantic. Marie Rollet, Pierre Desportes, Guillaume Couillard, Zacharie Cloutier of Normandy: not yet the famous filles du roi or the King's Daughters, only young people in provincial towns where the church recorded births in careful ink.

England was waking to America in the same years. Sir Humphrey Gilbert took letters from the queen in 1583 to plant English feet on "remote and barbarous" shores. Walter Raleigh named Virginia for the Virgin Queen in 1584. That same spring, Susan Riddlesdale was born to John and Dorcas Riddlesdale; Thomas French Sr. and Thomas Hooker's people were entering the record; John Leavens Sr. would soon join the long roster of yeomen and dissenters who preferred risk to stagnation.

In 1588 — the year the Spanish Armada broke against English seas — Guillaume Couillard was born at Québec to André Couillard and Jeanne Basset, one more French life on a cliff above a river that froze solid half the year. Two worlds were filling with the same kind of people: farmers, merchants, ministers' sons, women who would marry across oceans without maps for what came after.

By 1590, Scotland's James VI was already hunting witches in a panic that would later cross the border when he became James I of England — a foreshadowing this story will meet again in Massachusetts. For now, the important fact is simpler: in 1575 the Goodwater doorway opened with Hébert, and in 1581 the Powers doorway opened with Alcock. Everything before was prologue. Everything after is the long walk toward Winifred.

segments.title: Atlantic Crossings
supplementalSources: National Park Service — Jamestown settlement survival statistics
Canadian Museum of History — Samuel de Champlain, founding of Québec
Dictionary of Canadian Biography — Louis Hébert, apothecary and first European farmer at Québec
New Netherland Institute — Henry Hudson and Dutch trading posts

blocks.text: The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares. Our people were still mostly in parish registers on both shores, but the water between them was filling with names that would matter.

In England the births came steady as rain. William Preston in Chesham in 1591. Edmund Rice in Berkhamsted in 1594. John Upham in Devon, John Bent in Hampshire, William Towne in Norfolk, Martha Blanchard and Arthur Gary — each a line on a page that would later cross the sea. On 6 January 1609, Thomas Barrett was born at Thornbury in Gloucestershire to Christopher Barrett and Elizabeth Clarke; his granddaughter would one day sit in a Boston jail accused of witchcraft. On 8 July 1605, Walter Powers Sr. was baptized at Stanton St. Quintin in Wiltshire — the Powers name that Edith would carry into Vermont and Iowa was already in the font.

Scotland's James VI published his Daemonologie in 1597, obsessed with witches and violent death. When Elizabeth died childless in 1603, he walked south as James I of England — one crown, two kingdoms, and a church he meant to hold together with a new Bible. The Hampton Court Conference of 1604 set translators to work on what would become the King James Version: English words where Latin had ruled, Puritan hopes half-met and half-denied.

France was moving faster on the river. Samuel de Champlain — soldier, cartographer, royal geographer to Henry IV — had seen the Caribbean and Tadoussac where the Saguenay meets the St. Lawrence. Louis Hébert, the Paris apothecary born in 1575, married Marie Rollet in 1601 and kept shop while men around him talked of Acadia and the north. Their children Guillaume and Guillemette were born in the city; their father's life was already bending toward kebec — the Algonquin word for where the river narrows.

In 1606 James I chartered the Virginia Company of London and the Plymouth Company, still calling the whole coast Virginia. April 1607: three ships entered Chesapeake Bay and founded Jamestown on a feverish marsh. By 1608, of more than five hundred colonists, roughly one in eight still stood alive. The Plymouth Company's Popham Colony on the Kennebec lasted a winter and failed — fire, hunger, a harsh exchange with local peoples — but fishermen kept coming for cod on the Grand Banks anyway.

blocks.caption: The Virginia Company of London charter granted the company lands along the American coast.

blocks.text: While Jamestown coughed itself hollow, Champlain stopped at the great rapids on 3 July 1608 and built where Cartier had once wintered. Louis Hébert came with Recollect friars and Jesuits, broke soil behind the Habitation, planted wheat and rye at the spring — the first European farmer in what would become Canada, not a fur trader counting beaver pelts but a man with a hoe. In Perche, Jean Guyon was born in 1592; Zacharie Cloutier, Jacques Gaudry, and the Giffards and Fourniers of Normandy were growing up in towns that would empty into the St. Lawrence within a generation.

The Dutch followed money, not monarchs. Henry Hudson, sailing for the Dutch East India Company in 1609, probed Delaware Bay and the river that would carry his name past the island where Manhattan would rise. By 1610 the Dutch had seasonal posts on the Hudson, Connecticut, and Delaware rivers — trading with Mohawks and Pequots, selling metal and buying pelts. Captain John Smith, back from Jamestown, mapped the coast from Maine to Cape Cod in 1614 and named the country New England as if naming could make it tame.

In Assington, Suffolk, John Winthrop and Thomas French Sr. lived in the same parish world — Winthrop the gentleman who would lead two thousand Puritans west, French the father whose sons would fight in Pequot country. Margaret Huntington was born in Cheshire in 1608; Thomas French married Susan Riddlesdale that September and baptized a son on St. Edmund's day. England was producing the stock of plantations: yeomen, dissenters, widows with courage, ministers' sons who could read Latin and track a plow.

Robert Giffard, a surgeon from Perche, saw Québec in 1621 and came home preaching land and contracts — three years' labor for a farm of your own. The Dutch West India Company claimed the coast from Cape Cod to the Delmarva Peninsula in 1621. Pilgrims who refused Dutch New Netherland took a Plymouth Company patent in 1619; in November 1620 they anchored off Cape Cod without a charter for where they actually landed and signed a compact in the cabin smoke. Plimoth Plantation — they called it plantation, not colony yet — was a foot on the shore Winthrop's people would pass ten years later.

Champlain fought beside Wendat and Algonquin allies at Sorel in 1610, arquebus against Mohawk canoes — the beaver wars politicized before our cousins' farms were cleared. In Paris and Perche, in Wiltshire and Suffolk, the registers kept filling: Ralph Shepard, John Leavens Sr., William Ward, Alice Harris, Thomas Green, Elizabeth Lynde, William Perkins, Priscilla Chase — names that sound like law firms until you see them on muster rolls and town meeting minutes.

When James I died in 1625, his son Charles inherited a kingdom short of money and long on pride — Ship Money, forced loans, knighthood fees, Parliament's teeth showing. The century's next act would be civil war in England and plantation wars in America. For this family, the Atlantic crossings of 1591–1625 laid the keel: Hébert farming behind Québec's walls, Barrett and Powers and Preston and Rice still in England but not for long, Cloutier and Guyon in France, Dutch traders on rivers our people would later border, and Jamestown's survivors proving only that the gamble could be survived — not that it could be gentle.

segments.title: Planting Towns
supplementalSources: Canadian Encyclopedia — Kirke brothers, surrender of Québec 1629
Massachusetts Historical Society — Winthrop Fleet and Great Migration
Library and Archives Canada — Seigneurial system, filles à marier
National Park Service — Pequot War, Mystic Fort massacre

blocks.text: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets. Two branches of one tree planted in the same generation, unaware of the Iowa wedding waiting at the far end.

In 1626, while failed Gloucester fishermen retreated to Naumkeag and renamed it Salem without the king's permission, registers on both shores kept recording names. On 1 January, John Douglas Alcock was born at Markfield to George Alcock and Anne Hooker. Deacon John Upham married Elizabeth Webb at Bicton on 1 November. Robert Smith was born at Boston, Lincolnshire; Solomon Johnson married Eleanor Crofts in London. In France: Françoise Morin at Gahard, Anne Cloutier at Mortagne on 19 June, Pierre Tremblay at Randonnai, Pierre Saint-Denis and Vivienne Brunel at Dieppe and Rouen, Etienne Dumay Sr. at Dieppe, Sébastienne Veillon near La Rochelle, Marguerite Couillard at Québec on 6 August to Guillaume Couillard and Guillemette Hébert. Champlain granted Louis Hébert more land along the Saint-Charles River and opened the farm country at Château-Richer across from Île d'Orléans. The Iroquois destroyed the Mohicans and turned north toward the river.

1627 brought death and structure in equal measure. Louis Hébert — fifty-two years old, Québec's first farmer — fell on January ice; widow Marie Rollet kept the farm with son-in-law Guillaume Couillard, who inherited half the estate and received another hundred acres on the Saint-Charles, driving the colony's first plow the next spring. Cardinal Richelieu dissolved old trading monopolies and chartered the Company of One Hundred Associates — four thousand settlers promised in fifteen years, Catholics only, Huguenots pushed toward English and Dutch colonies. The seigneurial system arrived: river-front lots in three-by-nine-mile manors, feudal obligations dressed as settlement policy. Robert Giffard sailed home to Perche to recruit. Jean Guay was born at Berneuil. Richard Sawtelle married Elizabeth Pople; Ralph Farnham Sr. married Alice Harris at Southampton. England and France were at war; La Rochelle's Huguenots starved through a fifteen-month siege.

blocks.caption: The seigneurial system granted river-front land to seigneurs who subdivided it for habitants — a framework that shaped French Canada for two centuries.

blocks.text: The Kirke brothers made 1628–1629 brutal for New France. English ships intercepted Giffard's fleet from Dieppe; Robert and Marie Renouard turned back. Cap Tourmente's farm was burned, livestock slaughtered, men killed. Champlain bluffed through a July demand for surrender, but by August 1629 Québec had one tub of roots for sixty-seven starving souls. On 9 August he yielded to David, Thomas, and Louis Kirke. Abraham Martin, Marguerite Langlois, and most settlers sailed for England; Marie Hébert Rollet refused to leave the ground her husband had broken. Jean Nicolet stayed with the Nipissing, blocking English trade. Nine-year-old Hélène Desportes landed in Dieppe with aunt Marguerite Martin. Jacques Ménard was born at Fontenay. Jean Bonneau married Catherine Poulet. Euphrosine Madeleine Nicolet was born — Métis daughter of Nicolet and Jeanne Bahmahmaadjimiwin Giisis. In England, John Endecott signed the New England Company grant; the London Plantation patented Salem; William Perkins took his B.A. at Christ's College.

March 1629: Charles I dismissed Parliament for eleven years. The Massachusetts Bay Company charter arrived in August — and crucially failed to require meetings in England. Cambridge investors voted to move the whole company to America. In October they elected John Winthrop governor while he was still in Lincolnshire. The Petition of Right, passed 7 June 1628, would later bind the restored Stuart throne. When Québec fell, the French colony was forgotten in the crossfire of European religion — a pattern that would repeat every time Paris and London quarreled.

blocks.caption: In April 1630 the Winthrop Fleet left Southampton — the Great Migration that would carry the Powers branch to Massachusetts Bay.

blocks.text: April 1630 changed the English branch forever. Winthrop led Puritans out of Boston, Lincolnshire, toward a patent running from the Merrimack to the Charles. Eleven ships, roughly two thousand souls, livestock and salted fear. Salem could not feed them; they shivered through a Charlestown winter, then crossed to Shawmut where William Blackstone offered his peninsula — Boston rose from that mud. George Alcock, butcher of Roxbury, brought Anne Hooker and four-year-old John Douglas. Thomas French Sr. and Susan Riddlesdale came on the Arabella with sons including Thomas Jr., who married Mary Scudamore in Boston. Thomas Lamb, Dr. Richard Palgrave, the Richardson brothers, Thomas Green Jr., Roger Williams on the Lyon — each a line in the Winthrop journals. Paul Vachon and Pierre Bonneau were born in France; Pierre Gaudin at Châtillon-sur-Seine; Pierre Maheu and Marie Anne Le Laboureur in Perche and Bayeux. Hugh Maxwell was born in Scotland. Chief Masconomet entertained the Arbella's officers; hundreds died of hunger and nor'easters before the Lyon arrived 16 September with grain and Winthrop's family.

1631–1633 filled the margins. Elizabeth Wooton sailed with the fleet; Nathaniel Gary and Agnes Bent were born in England. George Alcock became freeman and deacon at Dorchester. William Perkins arrived March 1632 on the William & Francis; Ralph Shepard married Thankslorde Perkins in London; John Leavens Sr. and Elizabeth Stebbins reached Roxbury in June. Thomas Hooker — Anne Alcock's brother — fled Laud's net aboard the Griffin with two hundred Puritans and took the Cambridge church. Galileo's Dialogue earned an Inquisition summons; Archbishop Laud accelerated the exodus. Québec returned to France by the Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye in March 1632; Guillaume Couillard fought Iroquois raids, piloted to Tadoussac, became churchwarden. Giffard recruited pilot Noël Langlois Sr. Pierre Duchesne was born at Mesnil. Champlain rebuilt Québec as a true ville — upper town for elites, lower town for merchants and sailors. Anne Ozanne Achon was born at La Rochelle. Toussaint Giroux at Réveillon. Nicholas Holt married Elizabeth Short. Mary French was born 2 March 1634 in Boston.

1634 was the heaviest year on the St. Lawrence since Champlain's habitation. Giffard brought thirty Perche families on four ships — Guyon the mason with wife Mathurine and seven children including Marie, Zacharie Cloutier the carpenter with son Jean, Drouin the brickmaker, Bélanger, Côté father and son, Caron brothers, pilot Langlois, fille à marier Françoise Garnier. They wintered inside Fort Saint-Louis while Drouin laid brick for Giffard's manor and a parish church. Zacharie and Jean Guyon signed servitude contracts: passage, livestock, 845 acres after three years' labor. Cloutier brought wife Saintes and children including Anne. Trois-Rivières was founded eighty miles upriver. Hélène Desportes returned with Abraham Martin and daughter Anne; she married Guillaume Hébert 1 October. Jean Nicolet reached Green Bay wearing silk meant for China. Marie Crevet, Anne Antoinette de Liercourt, Louis Marier — more French births. Measles appeared in a Huron village, probably carried by settler children.

England's 1634 harvest was Laud's persecution and Wood's New England's Prospect — a book that sold the frontier like real estate. Ralph Shepard was summoned before the High Commission and sailed with Thankslorde and daughter on the smallpox-ridden Abigail. William Ward married Elizabeth Storey and became a Sudbury pillar. William Preston, widowed of Elizabeth Sale, married Mary Seabrook. Thomas Barrett married Margaret Huntington. William Perkins made freeman and ensign at Roxbury. Deacon William Ward helped petition for Marlborough. Edward Preston, Daniel Preston, Walter Powers Sr. and Joane Newman, Thomas Green Sr., Mary and John Baldwin, John Johnson, William Towne with wife Joanna and daughter Martha, Phineas Upham — the 1635 ships read like a parish census: Hopewell, James, Confidence, Truelove, Christian, Elizabeth. John Upham left Weymouth pregnant with Elizabeth Webb and three children. Nicholas Holt's tannery rose at Newbury; Ralph Farnham Sr. barbered at Ipswich. Roger Preston settled there. Rev. Joseph Hull planted Weymouth. Champlain died Christmas Day 1635; Abraham Martin received the heights that would become the Plains of Abraham. Mathieu Côté and family sailed from Mortagne.

1636–1637 split the English line west and lit the Connecticut powder keg. Harvard opened in the Bay Colony — John Douglas Alcock would graduate there in 1646. Thomas Hooker drove a hundred congregants and 160 cattle to the Connecticut River, founding Hartford, Wethersfield, and Windsor with freeman rules looser than Massachusetts. William Preston the solicitor moved to New Haven — likely among Hooker's company. Sergeant Thomas French Jr. fought the Pequot War and received grants at Ipswich and later Salisbury. The General Court organized militia regiments 13 December 1636 — ancestor of the National Guard. Between July 1636 and September 1638 the Pequot War scarred the river valley: trader murders, Endecott's burned villages, spring raids on Connecticut towns, then the Mystic Massacre of 26 May 1637 — five hundred Pequot dead in a pre-dawn fort fire, Sassacus beheaded by Mohawk allies. Nicholas Holt walked forty miles to vote Winthrop over Vane. Anne Mattle Douglas was born in England. Joseph Hills, Rose Clarke, and Rebecca sailed on the Susan & Ellen.

blocks.caption: May 1637 — Connecticut towns raised a militia under Captain John Mason for the campaign that ended at Mystic Fort.

blocks.text: French cousins in those same years were marrying into stone and fear. Governor Montmagny replaced Champlain. Saintes Cloutier arrived with Pierre and Anne to join Zacharie. Robert Drouin married eleven-year-old Anne Cloutier 12 July 1637; Jean Nicolet married Marguerite Couillard in October and settled at Trois-Rivières as clerk, ennobled Sieur de Belleborne. Langlois received 253 acres at Beauport below Montmorency Falls. François Bélanger married Marie Guyon. Marie Crevet came as a fille à marier and married Robert Caron. Smallpox reached Huron country — prelude to the beaver wars that would empty villages. Sillery opened for Christian Algonquins at an eel-and-salmon cove. Catherine Fortier, Sébastien Gingras, Suzanne Debure, Jeanne Mignon, Charles Garnier — births in Normandy and Perche that would matter when the ships came later.

The late 1630s brought the second wave to Massachusetts. Edmund Rice and Thomasine Frost arrived 1638; Rice would lease from Harvard president Henry Dunster, buy a Sudbury farm, become magistrate. John Bent brought Martha Blanchard and five children including Agnes to Sudbury on the Confidence. Joseph Hills opened Charlestown's first woolen shop. Ensign William Perkins joined the Ancient and Honorable Artillery Company and surveyed the Plymouth boundary. Robert Smith came as Whittingham's indentured servant. Thomas French Sr. farmed Reedy Marsh at Ipswich. Elizabeth Sawtelle, the Richardson brothers at Malden, John Leavens's wife Elizabeth Stebbin died October 1638. In 1639: Walter Powers Jr. born at Essex — the name Edith Powers would carry; Joseph Barrett born at Braintree; Francis Gould a Boston watchman; Richard Robbins to Charlestown; William Ward and Deborah on the Diligent to Sudbury; Solomon Johnson's family to Sudbury; Arthur Gary freeman at Roxbury; Guillaume Hébert died at Québec, likely smallpox, leaving Hélène Desportes with young children; Noël Morin the cartwright arrived; Ursulines and Hospitalières landed with Madame de la Peltrie. Laurent Migneron, Martin Adroche Côté, Marguerite Langlois — Canada births and marriages. Françoise Hébert, Nicolas Bélanger, Anne Lemoine, Louis Robert.

1640–1642: towns hardened and empires cracked. John Leavens Jr., Tryall Shepard, Joshua Lamb, Elizabeth Perkins, Roger Preston and Martha Blessing Towne, John Ramsdell and Priscilla Chase, William Douglas the cooper with Ann Mattle and daughter Anne — each a certificate of planting. William Preston sat first in New Haven's meetinghouse. By 1640 forty thousand English lived in the Bay Colony. Noël Morin married Hélène Desportes 9 January with her house near Notre-Dame and three stepchildren. Nicolas Bonhomme married fille à marier Catherine Gouge. Jean Carrier was born at Saintes; Jean Baptiste Halay married Mathurine Valet at Chartres-de-Bretagne. Michel Messier born at Rouen. Jeanne Mance and Paul de Maisonneuve recruited forty-eight souls and founded Ville-Marie at the island confluence — future Montréal. Jean Nicolet drowned in the St. Lawrence 27 October 1642 after canoeing from Lake Nipissing. King Charles I's quarrel with Parliament became civil war; Winthrop noted immigration fall from two thousand a year to a trickle. John Upham signed a Weymouth land treaty with local Natives April 1642.

blocks.caption: In 1637 Rev. Thomas Hooker and his congregation walked cattle 160 miles west to plant the Connecticut towns.

blocks.text: 1643–1648: the beaver wars began in earnest. Iroquois with Dutch firearms raided Ville-Marie and Trois-Rivières; Canada counted roughly three hundred fifty souls against twelve thousand Haudenosaunee. Jean Dumay arrived with sons Etienne and André; Jean Leblanc married Métis Euphrosine Nicolet to Guillaume Couillard's household. Guillaume Couillard built a lime kiln. Mathieu Choret served Jesuits at Trois-Rivières. Jacques Ménard, Vincent Chrétien, Jean Baptiste Bourgery, Jacques Samson, Anne Dequain, Jeanne Renard — more arrivals and births in France and Québec. Nicholas Holt moved to Andover with his tannery and ferry; William Perkins became Captain of Weymouth militia. Woburn held its first town meeting 13 April 1644. Ville-Marie imported mastiff bitches named Pilote to scent ambushes. Louis Gagne and Marie Michel left Perche. François Bélanger and Masse Gravel farmed Beaupré coast. Cromwell's New Model Army won Naseby 1645; the Little Ice Age bit New England farms. Barbe Halay born at Chartres-de-Bretagne; Marie Anne Leclerc at Dieppe. Noël Morin received Saint-Luc lands; Jean Côté got title after ten years' work. Guyon and Cloutier led a Corpus Christi procession in Québec — bell-ringers, torches, mason's compass on a shield.

1647 poured French settlers like a second spring: Raymond Pagé with Madeleine Bergeron and the colony's first horse; Pierre Tremblay enlisted for three years; Charlotte Gaudry and sons Nicolas and Jacques; Thomine Bigot's orphaned Françoise married Charles Guillebourg within weeks of arrival; Mathieu Choret returned with wife Sébastienne Veillon; Gervais le Normand and family from Mortagne; Ignace Bonhomme born at Québec. William Preston's pew order at New Haven; Nicholas Holt laid Andover's bounds; Edward Rice married Agnes Bent at Sudbury; Daniel Cheever and Esther Hester reached Cambridge. Anne Cloutier Drouin died 4 February 1648; Robert Drouin remarried widow Marie Chapelier and moved toward Trois-Rivières fur trade. Etienne Dumay Sr. married fille à marier Françoise Morin and pushed toward Ville-Marie. Marie Hébert Rollet — first European woman farmer, first teacher at Québec — died May 1649. The Iroquois shattered the Huron; four Jesuits died with them. Margaret Jones, Charlestown midwife, hanged 1648 — first of fifteen New England witch executions before 1693.

1649–1651: Charles I lost his head; Cromwell ruled Britain; Scottish prisoners including David and John Stirling sailed on the John and Sarah as indentured servants. Francis Gould married Rose and moved toward Braintree. Pierre Bonneau married Marie Lambert; Joseph Bonneau born at Vernoux. Anne Saint-Denis, Etiennette Loriot, Jacques Dion, Catherine Rivet, Antoine Dubois, Mathurine Goard, Anne Le Seigneur. John Eliot, backed by Cromwell's £12,000, organized Praying Indian villages — Nashoba granted 1651 for Chief Tahattawan's people, land that would become Littleton after King Philip's War. Nathaniel Richardson born at Woburn; Edward Preston married Margaret Hurst at New Haven. Ville-Marie shrank below fifty under Mohawk attack; Paul de Maisonneuve sailed home to recruit or abandon. Trois-Rivières bled — Father Buteux killed, governor and twenty-two settlers dead August 1651. Pierre Maheu contracted to Juchereau; Guillaume Fournier arrived; Blaise Juillet married fille à marier Anne Antoinette de Liercourt. Guillaume Labelle born at Lisieux; Nicole Dumay born at Sillery.

1652–1653: the Grande Rescue gathered on the horizon. Mary Alcock baptized at Roxbury; Nathaniel Gary admitted to church; Thomas Green Jr. married Rebecca Hills at Malden. Zacharie Cloutier received Château-Richer land from Governor Lauzon. Jean Guay married fille à marier Jeanne Mignon at Point Lévis. Jean Baptiste Bourgery sailed from La Rochelle. Paul Vachon the mason-notary married Marguerite Langlois at Beauport. Pierre Gaudin enlisted as master carpenter, advanced 127 livres, arrived September 1653. On 16 November, Maisonneuve landed one hundred recruits at Ville-Marie — the settlement would live. Nicolas Gaudry married twelve-year-old Agnés Morin. Toussaint Giroux contracted with Giffard. Jean Bonneau — Huguenot, first of the Bonneau-Goodwater line — reached Canada; Catherine Hurault and Jean Lemarché came with the rescue to Montréal. Michel Messier and Jacques-Hugues Picard among the soldiers. Noël Morin became Sieur de Saint-Luc with river-front seigneurie. William Peacock married Mary Willis at Roxbury.

1654–1655 closed the era with land, law, and loss. Joane Newman Powers sailed with fifteen-year-old Walter Jr. after Walter Sr.'s death — Salem landing for the branch that would reach Vermont and Iowa. John Upham, Ralph Shepard, and Joseph Hills angered the General Court by electing their own minister at Malden. Martha Gould born at Braintree. Lt. Quentin Moral married widow Marie Marguerie Hertel and fought over inherited acres at Trois-Rivières. Pierre Gaudin and Jeanne Rousseliere married at Ville-Marie; Jean Lemarché married Catherine Hurault; Toussaint Giroux married Marie Godard under Giffard's roof — twin infants dead within a week. Guillaume Couillard ennobled by Louis XIV. Jean Bonneau summoned son Pierre to Île d'Orléans; Paul Vachon laid Hôtel-Dieu stone with Governor Lauzon. Robert Drouin, prosperous at last, returned to Château-Richer with Marie Chapelier and brick for the Jesuits. May 1655: Chelmsford approved on Pennacook land — salmon falls, maple sugar, beaver meadows handed to English farmers who found fields already cleared. Elizabeth Preston born at New Haven; Daniel Rice Sr. at Sudbury; John Preston at Ipswich. Pierre Saint-Denis and Jean Baptiste Halay sailed for Québec, leaving wives and children in France. Paul Vachon notarized at Notre-Dame-des-Anges. Noël Morin bought a cathedral pew for 1.7 acres. Bourgery's family sheltered inside Trois-Rivières fort as Iroquois pressure mounted.

Between 1626 and 1655 the family learned two vocabularies of survival: freeman oaths and militia drills in towns named for English shires, and contrats de servitude, filles à marier, and Jesuit bells above a river that froze six months a year. English villages multiplied toward forty thousand souls; French Canada counted hundreds, not thousands — yet the Québec farm, the Beauport manor, the Ville-Marie rescue, and the Bonneau field on Île d'Orléans would outlast every charter. The next segment is frontier and faith — colder winters, louder wars, and prayers in three languages on soil that was never empty.

segments.title: Frontier and Faith
supplementalSources: Canadian War Museum — Carignan-Salières Regiment and Tracy campaign

blocks.text: After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp. Faith meant different things on each shore — Puritan meetinghouses where Eliot's Praying Indians wore English coats, and stone chapels where Jesuits exorcised devils from teenage girls. Both branches were about to learn that frontier peace is always borrowed time.

1656–1658 kept the registers busy on both sides of the ocean. Edward Peter Hurst died in Boston; his daughter Margaret had married Edward Preston at New Haven in 1651. William Towne sold Salem land and bought Topsfield meadow. Robert Smith, tailor of Rowley, married Mary French. Eleven-year-old William Taylor came alone on the Speedwell toward country that would become Marlborough. In Canada, Robert Drouin's prosperity showed in dowries — from nothing in 1656 to milk cows, clothing, and livres cash within eight years. Marie Anne Le Laboureur arrived from Bayeux as a fille à marier and married carpenter Jean Normand in Charlesbourg's star-shaped Jesuit layout. Ignace Gagne was born at Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré; Étienne Dumay Jr. at Sillery; Marie Bonneau followed her husband Pierre from France with seven-year-old Joseph. Louis XIV, recalling Governor Lauzon, authorized the "36-mois" indenture — three years' labor, no citizenship, no fur trade, hope of land at the end. Canada held fewer than three thousand souls against an empire of beaver and fear.

Deacon Ralph Mousall of Charlestown left £20 in his will to ward Ruth Wood — who would marry Phineas Upham at Malden on 14 February 1658. Nathaniel Gary wed Anne Mattle Douglas at Roxbury; Walter Powers Jr. would marry Tryall Shepard three years later. John Johnson and Deborah Ward married at Sudbury; Bethiah Ward born to Deacon William Ward and Elizabeth Storey — fifteen children between them. Solomon Johnson received 130 acres in Sudbury's two-mile strip. Between 1651 and 1658, John Eliot and Daniel Gookin organized seven Praying Indian villages — over fourteen such towns eventually, forty-five to sixty Native inhabitants each, Bibles in Algonquin, English houses, Puritan meetinghouses. Chief Tahattawan's Nashoba grant of 1651 would one day become Littleton. Oliver Cromwell died in 1658; son Richard lasted months before exile. The Act of Uniformity marked every non-Anglican a nonconformist. David and John Stirling, Scottish prisoners indentured in New England, sailed home when the Protectorate cracked.

French Canada in those same years filled with arrivals and plague. On 8 March 1658, Étienne de Lessard donated land for a chapel at Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré — rheumatic builder Louis Guimont cured laying three foundation stones, miracles multiplying. Michel Messier married fille à marier Anne Lemoine at Ville-Marie and bought Île de Montréal acreage. Léonard Pilote sailed from La Rochelle on Le Taureau, leaving pregnant Denise Gauthier behind; by October he was procurator for merchant Michel Desorcy, emissary of the king. Toussaint Giroux finished servitude under Giffard, built a heated room with cellar and attic at Beauport, took up eel fishing beside Michel Baugis. In 1659, Bishop François de Laval arrived with Paul de Rainville's family and eighty-four Normandy fishermen. Jeanne Mance recruited Olivier Charbonneau's laboring family — the Saint-André lost its rudder, returned, sailed again, and the Black Plague killed ten at sea; the infection spread through Québec until Charbonneau fled to Boucherville with Pierre Dagenets, wheat farming and a water mill at Pointe-aux-Trembles. Paul Vachon became notary at Beauport and first notary of Île d'Orléans. Jean Pichet contracted at Château-Richer. Pierre Maheu contracted to marry twelve-year-old Jeanne Drouin before notary Paul Vachon. Léonard Pilote wrote Denise: "It is here you must come. I have obtained a beautiful grant right next door to Québec."

30 January 1660: Charles II climbed to a throne his father had lost on a scaffold. The Restoration bargain gave him army and purge; Parliament held him to the Petition of Right — no forced loans, no martial law without consent. Charles II and his ministers pulled colonies under crown control and passed Navigation Acts forcing Atlantic trade onto English hulls, turning colonial merchants into smugglers overnight. In Massachusetts, the General Court named Marlborough and ordered every claimant to pay public charges by November or forfeit interest — Edmund Rice, William Ward, Solomon Johnson, John Howe among the signers. William Ward moved nine miles west from Sudbury, became Marlborough's first deacon, received fifty acres. Roger Preston kept a Salem inn for strangers and strong liquor. William Sterling married Elizabeth Sawtelle at Haverhill — ship carpenter, miller, future selectman. Francis Gould and Rose Whitehall left Braintree for Chelmsford's western edge in late 1659. William Douglas bought at New London and moved from Boston with Ann Mattle and their children. In Canada, Jean Carrier arrived from Saintes to work for the Hospitallers; Nicolas Bélanger married Marie de Rainville at Beauport; Iroquois raiders took girls from Ville-Marie — Marie Madeleine Bourgery, Jeanne Baillargeon — and slaughtered militia who ventured beyond the walls.

1661 was a year of terror on the St. Lawrence. Walter Powers Jr. and educated Tryall Shepard bought land from Lt. Joseph Wheeler north of Concord's garrison, built on Quagany Hill. Joseph-François Hertel, stepson of Lt. Quentin Moral, was captured by four Iroquois, tortured, adopted, smuggled letters to Father Simon Le Moyne, escaped — future "Hero of Canada." Louis Gagne died with seven Frenchmen on the Mohawk River near future Auriesville. Michel Messier was reported burned by Onondagas; he would surface again. Iroquois massacred nearly a hundred French and Huron on Île d'Orléans; Sainte-Famille parish rose from the blood. Governor Davaugour sent Pierre Boucher to France; Louis XIV chose the Carignan-Salières Regiment — hand-picked soldiers fresh from fighting Turks — rather than abandon the colony. Raymond Pagé moved his carpenter's family to Beaupré waterfront; Jacques Lareau sharecropped north of the Saint-Charles as carpenter and wood-turner. Michele Picard born at Ville-Marie to Jacques-Hugues Picard and widow Anne Antoinette de Liercourt. Charles Garnier sailed from Caen. Marie-Marthe Moral born at Trois-Rivières where Quentin Moral would argue 29 cases in a court that tried 907 disputes for 700 settlers — two-thirds debt, inheritance the rest.

1662–1663 restructured both empires. Louis XIV made Canada a royal province; stone replaced boat-builder timber; upper towns and lower towns plotted on paper before ground was broken. The filles à marier program ended — 262 women in twenty-eight years, average ten per year. Jean Besset of Cahors joined La Tour's Company of the coming regiment. Jean Pichet bought 38.5 acres at Saint-Pierre on Île d'Orléans — one of three hundred founding families. Iroquois killed Jean Leblanc at Saint-Pierre; widow Euphrosine Nicolet remarried Elie Dussault. Denise Pilote arrived with children Marguerite and Jean. Thomas Barrett and Margaret Huntington left Braintree for Chelmsford in 1663 — fifty-two acres on Robin's Hill, great central chimney, low ceilings against cold. On 5 February, earthquakes shook the valley; Jesuit Father Lalemant counted shocks morning through night; a glacial moraine failed and sent a wall of water down the St. Lawrence, scouring lower Québec. Jean Guyon died mid-quake after twenty-six years in Canada; his estate quarreled five years. Angiers warriors killed eight at Beaupré; François Bélanger spared, prospering toward forty-two acres and thirteen cattle. The Conseil souverain ended the Company of One Hundred Associates. Louis XIV launched the filles du Roi — parish certificates, priest's recommendation, dowries paid by merchants at fifty livres per passage. Thirty-eight arrived 22 September 1663. Michel Messier escaped the Iroquois again.

1664–1665 brought plague to London and soldiers to Québec. Bubonic death climbed from 43 in May to 31,159 in August — red crosses on doors, "Bring out your dead," Charles II fled to Oxford while fifteen percent of England perished. English ships seized Dutch New Netherland and split it into New York and New Jersey; Iroquois lost Dutch gun suppliers but kept the English alliance. Charles Garnier married fille à marier Louise Vezina at Château-Richer — six children would die young. Florian LeClerc died at Trois-Rivières, leaving Marie Gendre seven children including Marie Madeleine Bourgery, Iroquois captive rehabilitated by Ursuline sisters. Then the Carignan-Salières Regiment arrived — twelve hundred soldiers delayed at La Rochelle for Huguenot conversions and canoe-building, landed June through September under Tracy. Forts rose on the Richelieu; Mohawk country felt French steel. Jean Talon came as intendant. Léonard Pilote died at Beauport 26 May. René Dumats and Mathieu Gervais landed on L'Aigle d'Or; Jean Besset on Le Vieux Simêon after a hundred-day crossing; Louis Robert billeted with Marie Gendre's household in the attacked fort at Trois-Rivières. Filles du Roi Anne Bellesoeur married Nicolas Massard; Catherine Rivet would marry Pierre Duchesne. Vincent Chrétien, whose brothers Louis XIV had beheaded, settled Sainte-Famille on Île d'Orléans. Michel Messier became Sieur de Saint-Michel with fifty acres and militia lieutenant's commission. Noël Langlois Sr. sheltered regiment officers — François Laumonier de Traversy killed hunting Lake Champlain; young Noël Jr. would take the dit name Traversy.

blocks.caption: Iroquois raids pushed French settlers behind palisades — the beaver wars that made the Carignan-Salières campaign necessary.

blocks.text: 1666 was defeat, census, and marriage in the same breath. Governor Courcelle led six hundred Carignan soldiers into Mohawk country in January snowshoes without Algonquin guides — lost, exhausted, stumbled into Schenectady in New York. Mohawk warriors ambushed French officers at Lake Champlain in July; Tracy imprisoned a peace delegation. Fall brought thirteen hundred men under Tracy destroying deserted Mohawk villages and seizing crops; Chief Canaqueese carried peace terms into 1667. Jean Talon's door-to-door census counted 3,215 Canadians in 538 families — 2,034 men, 1,181 women, professions from servants to gentlemen. Laurent Migneron and Anne Saint-Denis married at Château-Richer, signing with X's, starting in a wooden shack. Louis Robert married twelve-year-old Marie Madeleine Bourgery in Trois-Rivières, working as cobbler while she remained with her mother. At the Hôtel-Dieu, Jean Carrier met Barbe Halay — Bishop Laval's ward, "possessed by the devil" until exorcism succeeded; she would work for the sisters before their cathedral wedding. Jean Pichet married Marie Madeleine Leblanc on Île d'Orléans — her mother Euphrosine, Métis daughter of Jean Nicolet. Pierre Duchesne wed Catherine Rivet at Montmorency. Denise Pilote assigned Jesuit domestic Jean Drouard guardian of ten-year-old Jean Pilote. Paul Vachon raised seven cattle on twenty acres while serving as seigneurial attorney and notary on Île d'Orléans.

blocks.caption: Fort Chambly on the Richelieu River — wooden predecessor built in 1665 when Jean Besset and the Carignan companies wintered in Québec.

blocks.text: Peace let the colony breathe and the king's daughters fill the empty beds. Four hundred Carignan soldiers stayed in 1668; Louis XIV granted a hundred francs and a year's food to each. At Fort Chambly, Jean Besset married fille du Roi Anne Le Seigneur — officer Jacques d'Harcinval witnessing, best man Jean-Baptiste de Poitiers, winter at Fort Saint-Louis above Québec. Vincent Chrétien wed Marie Anne Leclerc at Sainte-Famille. Lt. Michel Messier received the fief Cap de la Trinité with brother-in-law Jacques Le Moyne. Louis XIV sent twelve mares, two stallions, fifty sheep, and 64,000 livres to recruit five hundred more souls. Nicolas Gaudry died leaving Agnés Morin eight children; neighbor Jacques Nourry executed for molesting a child — his land passed to Charles Garnier. Jeanne Mance wiped Ville-Marie debts. Olivier Charbonneau finally repaid Jeanne Mance's passage loan after ten years. Joseph Bonneau finished mason's servitude under Noël Jérémie, leased land on Île d'Orléans, bought 2.5 acres with cabin and outdoor oven, partnered with Pierre Letat sawing timbers for Intendant Talon. On 31 October 1670 he married fille du Roi Marie-Anne Lelong at Sainte-Famille with joint property and a 250-livre dowry. Four ships brought 150 filles du Roi and tons of supplies that August. Jean Carrier and exorcised Barbe Halay wed in the cathedral 4 November. Zacharie Cloutier sold Beauport and crossed the river to Château-Richer.

2 May 1670: Charles II's cousin Prince Rupert chartered the Hudson's Bay Company — oldest joint-stock merchandiser in the English-speaking world, exclusive trade on all rivers draining into Hudson Bay, vast Rupert's Land named for the prince. Two French traders had pitched the idea to England after Paris refused them in 1665; beaver felt hats in Europe turned pelts into empire. While English merchants looked north, French cousins married south and west. Guillaume Labelle, illiterate and confirmed at Ville-Marie in 1668, courted fifteen-year-old Anne Charbonneau in November 1671 with notaries, Sulpicians, and lords of Longueuil present — 300 livres from the bride, land bought from surgeon and ex-Carignan soldier René Sauvageau payable in roof planks. Jacques Samson wed Marie Anne Metru days after she arrived from Paris with father Claude — her mother dead at childbirth. René Dumats married fille du Roi Marie Lelong. Agnés Morin, widowed of Nicolas Gaudry, married Ignace Bonhomme with eight children in tow. Noël Morin rendered homage for his Saint-Luc seigneurie on the Richelieu. John Ramsdell Jr. and Elizabeth Perkins married at Boxford and joined Leonard brothers' iron works — first castings in New England. William Taylor wed Mary Johnson at Marlborough; she died in childbirth December 1672, infant Samuel following weeks later.

blocks.caption: Jean Carrier and Barbe Halay married at the Cathédrale de Québec in 1670 — faith, hospital, and frontier in one ceremony.

blocks.text: 1672–1673 tightened the frontier's net. Governor Frontenac arrived contentious and ceremonious, clashing with the Sovereign Council. Joseph Barrett married Martha Gould at Chelmsford 17 September 1672 — Goulds and Barretts already tied by Francis Gould's daughter. Sergeant Thomas French Jr. won Pequot War veterans' land northeast of Salisbury with eight Ipswich men. William Douglas became New London deacon and General Court deputy. Jean Lebeau married fille du Roi Etiennette Loriot at Chambly. Noël Langlois Jr. wed Aimée Caron — stepdaughter of his father's second wife Marie Crevet; he took Traversy as dit name and followed Frontenac west toward Kingston, earning a seigneury called Port Joli for oak spars and hunting ground. Guillaume Fournier received thirty acres on the south shore — pioneer of Montmagny parish, church later built on three acres he donated. Louis XIV ordered uncleared seigneurial lands forfeited to the crown; Noël Morin cleared Saint-Luc and parceled to sons and stepchildren. Rebecca Barrett born at Chelmsford 4 July 1673. That October the town approved Joseph Barrett and Francis Gould building a sixteen-by-eighteen-foot lookout on Robin's Hill — single-thickness boards, one window, watchers for hostile warriors. Jean Besset received twenty-six arpents on the Richelieu at Chambly, then sold and bought sixty-eight acres at Sault St-Louis opposite Ville-Marie. Québec legislated latrines and ended the French habit of throwing waste into streets.

1674 was births and land transfers before the storm. John Leavens Jr. married Elizabeth Preston at New Haven — second wife after Hannah Woods died at Roxbury. Walter Powers III born at Concord to Walter Jr. and Tryall. Marie Suzanne Samson born at Pointe-Lévy; Pierre Julien Dion at Québec. Joseph Bonneau paid debts in silver. Guillaume Labelle and Olivier Charbonneau accepted Pierre Boucher's offer to farm forty-five cultivated acres on Île Jésus — oxen, hay, wheat, poultry — sharecroppers poised to become proprietors. In England, Richard Sawtelle's Groton garrison had sheltered neighbors since the 1660s; he would petition out of militia training at sixty. The Nashoba Praying Village still held Chief Tahattawan's people on land granted 1651 — three by four miles that English maps would later call Littleton. Faith had bought a generation of uneasy coexistence. It would not buy the next.

blocks.caption: 1675 — tribal lands across New England as King Philip's War ignited; Marlborough and Chelmsford sat on the frontier line.

blocks.text: 1675 exploded. King Philip — Metacomet, son of the Mayflower's ally Massasoit — led Nipmuck, Pocumtuc, and Narragansett warriors against towns that had been eating his people's land for fifty years. Twelve towns destroyed, half of New England attacked, one in ten military-age men dead. Phineas Upham commissioned lieutenant in September, marched Nipmuck country with Captain Gorham, joined the Massachusetts regiment under Captain Isaac Johnson. On 19 December, Governor Josiah Winslow's united army attacked the Great Swamp fort in Rhode Island — frozen swamp ice allowing approach where finished walls would have made assault suicide. Narragansett and Wampanoag defenders burned in their cedar island stronghold; Nathaniel Richardson of Woburn, in Captain Prentiss's horse troop, was among the wounded. John Preston served under Captain Gardner. Deacon William Douglas delivered provisions to the commissary. At Marlborough, William Taylor garrisoned with Deacon William Ward — whose house was burned, cattle killed, a son killed in the fighting. Joshua Lamb married Mary Alcock at Roxbury. Daniel Johnson born to John Johnson and Deborah Ward at Marlborough. Mary Ramsdell born at Topsfield. Laurent Migneron's wife Anne Saint-Denis died in childbirth; Paul Vachon inventoried a shack, two cows, two bulls, veal calves, a sow — half the land to the widow, half to three children before Laurent remarried fille du Roi widow Marie Guillaume in May.

Between 1656 and 1675 the family stood on two frontiers learning the same lesson in different tongues. English cousins pushed garrison houses, lookout shacks, and militia drills west of Boston while Praying Villages that Eliot had built with Cromwell's money faced starvation on Deer Island. French cousins survived plague ships, earthquakes, Iroquois terror, regiment demobilization, and filles du Roi dowries to raise stone chimneys on Île d'Orléans and Richelieu riverlots. The Hudson's Bay Company and the Carignan muster roll were the empires' answers to the same beaver — trade and troops where settlement alone failed. King Philip's War was not the end of the story; it was the crack in the ice. The next segment is wars and witch panic — burned towns giving way to burned women, and a French colony that thought peace would last a decade.
